exemplar.title: An investigation on finding the activation energy for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide catalysed by Fe 3+ ions present in Iron (III) Chloride.

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation on finding the activation energy for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide catalysed by Fe 3+ ions present in Iron (III) Chloride.

strengths: Raw data and processed tables are presented with clear headings and uncertainty notation.
Apparatus uncertainties are communicated precisely in a dedicated table.
Arrhenius plot is well-presented with trendline and caption, facilitating Ea determination.
weaknesses: Uncertainties are not propagated through to rate constants or the calculated activation energy.
Rate constant units (sâ»Â¹) are omitted, and EA units are inconsistently handled (J vs kJ).
The Arrhenius graph omits two data points (318 K and 323 K), weakening the robustness of the linear fit.
The rate-equation form contains a miswritten double negative exponent.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording is clear and precise (band 5), processing shows some inaccuracies (band 4), and uncertainties are considered but not fully addressed (band 3). Averaging these strands leads to a holistic score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: The research question is situated within a specific realâworld context linking biological catalysis to industrial water treatment.
Clear justification of the gas-syringe method over mass-loss demonstrates thoughtful methodological choice.
Independent, dependent and controlled variables are comprehensively identified (especially in Table 3).
Theoretical framework is supported by a well-labelled MaxwellâBoltzmann distribution figure and caption.
weaknesses: No justification is provided for selecting a 50 cmÂ³ oxygen endpoint.
Tables 2 and 5 are empty or broken, reducing clarity in variable specification.
Procedure steps contain ambiguities (e.g. gas-syringe pressure maintenance and heater volume details).
Inconsistent significant figures and column alignment in some tables could lead to misinterpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands clearly achieve the 5â6 band (contextualisation and methodological considerations), while the methodology description has minor ambiguities placing it at the top of the 3â4 band. Overall performance merits a holistic score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Conclusion correctly links increased temperature to faster reaction rates and reports an activation energy value consistent with the data.
Includes a literature comparison and percentage error calculation for the experimentally determined Ea.
weaknesses: The wording incorrectly states that the rate decreases with catalyst addition instead of increasing.
Comparison to a generic literature value lacks specificity to FeClâ catalysis.
Discussion of exothermicity and its relation to activation energy is conceptually muddled.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is consistent with the data but contains conceptual errors (band 4) and makes only some relevant but insufficiently justified comparisons to scientific context (band 3). A holistic score of 3 out of 6 is appropriate.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (e.g. HâOâ decomposition during waiting) are clearly identified.
Evaluation provides a balanced reflection on experimental design and data reliability.
weaknesses: Suggested improvements are generic (e.g. compare different catalysts) and not explicitly linked to error reduction.
The quantitative impact of methodological issues on rate constants or Ea is not explained.
Only one realistic improvement is described in depth, others lack detail.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate describes specific weaknesses and gives realistic but under-developed improvements (band 3 for weaknesses, band 2 for improvements). Overall performance fits the mid-band (3â4) with limited depth in improvement discussion, yielding a holistic score of 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and is embedded in a specific, real-world context (water disinfection and the industrial use of FeClâ to catalyse HâOâ decomposition). The introduction links biological catalase, environmental water treatment and transition-metal catalysis, showing the student understands why the investigation is relevant. This level of contextualisation aligns with the top-band description.

Processing includes means, rates, ln k, reciprocal temperatures and a linear regression to obtain Ea. Calculations are largely correct, though the student equates 1/t to k and carries forward that approximation; units are not shown for ln k. These constitute âsome significant inaccuraciesâ, so the top of the 3â4 band is appropriate.

Raw data are organised in clear tables with correct units and uncertainties; processed data and the Arrhenius graph are presented cleanly. Although table headers are duplicated once, the overall presentation is both clear and precise, justifying a high-band award (score 5 as slight formatting issues prevent full marks).

One realistic improvement (prepare fresh HâOâ before each trial) is stated and briefly described, but other suggestions are vague (âcompare different catalystsâ) and not linked to error reduction. The work therefore sits in the 1â2 band, with both suggestions present earning the higher score within that band.

Instrument uncertainties are tabulated and percentage errors calculated, but they are not propagated through the rate constants or the activation-energy value. No combined uncertainty for Ea is presented. Hence there is âevidence of consideration â¦ with some significant omissionsâ, matching the 3â4 band at the lower end.

The student compares the experimental Ea to an approximate literature value for âmetal catalystsâ (45 kJ molâ»Â¹) and calculates percentage error, but notes that the reference may be unsuitable. This constitutes âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than a fully justified one, meriting a mid-band (score 3).

Specific weaknesses (decomposition of HâOâ while waiting, limited control of concentration) are described, but the relative impact on Ea or rate is not explained in depth. This matches the description of the 3â4 band at a mid-level score.

Independent, dependent and controlled variables are identified in detailed tables; the choice of the gas-syringe method, temperature range and constant catalyst concentration is explained. The student justifies why 30 Â°C is the lowest attainable temperature and why mass-loss was rejected. Minor gaps remain (e.g. no justification for 50 cmÂ³ gas end-point), but overall the methodological considerations are thoroughly explained, matching the 5â6 band; a score of 5 reflects the small omissions.

The numbered procedure together with the apparatus list is sufficient for another student to replicate the work, but some ambiguities persist: step 3 confuses volumes placed on the heater, the method for ensuring constant pressure in the gas syringe is not described, and there is no clarification of how temperature is maintained during gas collection. These minor ambiguities place the work at the top of the 3â4 band.

The conclusion restates the main findings and is generally consistent with the processed data (higher T â faster rate; Ea reduced to 32 kJ molâ»Â¹). However, the discussion of exothermicity is conceptually muddled and does not fully reconcile with the analysis. Thus the strand fits the 3â4 band, upper end.

annotations.comment: The student situates the research question within a clear context of biological catalysis and industrial water treatment, demonstrating a strong understanding of why FeÂ³âº ions are relevant to HâOâ decomposition.
The research question is embedded in a specific scenario, but the rationale for selecting 50 cmÂ³ of Oâ as the endpoint is not justified, which weakens the methodological justification.

The introduction effectively links catalaseâs role in biology to industrial water treatment, showing thoughtful context and relevance of transition-metal catalysis before presenting the specific question.
The background section ends abruptly with an incomplete citation (â(ib academyâ), suggesting OCR or editing errors that interrupt clarity in methodological explanation.
Figure 1 and its caption clearly illustrate the MaxwellâBoltzmann distribution and the effect of catalysts on Eâ, supporting the theoretical framework underpinning the hypothesis.
The choice of the gas-syringe method is well justified versus mass loss, emphasizing reliability. However, the endpoint volume of 50 cmÂ³ lacks a clear justification for its selection.
The hypothesis identifies expected decrease in Eâ with FeClâ catalyst, but it would be strengthened by stating an estimated numerical change or relative magnitude based on literature.
The raw data table for trial times is well organized with clear headings and uncertainty notation, facilitating precise data recording and subsequent analysis.
Table 5âs heading is empty and the table layout appears broken, limiting clarity in variable identification. Ensure all tables have complete, labeled entries.
Dependent variable description appears fragmented within Table 1, with explanatory text extending beyond the cell. Reorganize into coherent sentences and ensure variables are distinct.
Table 3 controlled variables comprehensively lists concentration, volume, and control methods, enabling reproducibility and showing good methodological consideration.
Table 2 is empty with no controlled variables specified. This omission weakens the systematic identification of all relevant controls.
Apparatus uncertainties are tabulated clearly, including instruments, uncertainty magnitudes, and quantities, reflecting precise communication of measurement precision.
Chemical volumes and concentrations in Table 4 lack consistency in significant figures and column alignment, which could cause misinterpretation of reagent details.
The risk assessment section articulates human and environmental hazards and mitigations clearly, demonstrating thorough safety consideration.
Step 1 of preparation omits description of how back-pressure in the filled-inverted syringe is maintained or monitored, which could introduce systematic error.
Step 3 uses separate heaters for HâOâ and FeClâ solutions, risking temperature mismatch. A single thermostatic bath or pre-equilibration could improve control.
Rate calculation uses Rate = 1/time but omits explicit units for k, which should be stated as sâ»Â¹ for clarity and precision.
Processed data table presents rate constants clearly, supporting subsequent Arrhenius plot calculations and demonstrating precise data handling.
Coordinates table for Arrhenius graph includes only three points (303â313 K), omitting data at 318 and 323 K, which reduces the robustness of the linear fit.
The qualitative observations are brief; richer detail on foam volume, bubble size, or solution clarity at each temperature would strengthen interpretation.
Uncertainty propagation is absent in the average time calculation; incorporate instrument error into mean time to quantify its effect on rate constants.
Reference list contains broken URLs and inconsistent citation formatting; ensure all sources are correctly formatted and accessible.
The Arrhenius plot caption and graph are well presented with trendline, facilitating clear visual determination of EA.
Equation 1/t = Ae^{-Ea/RT} is miswritten as containing a double negative exponent. Correct form is k = Aâe^{-Ea/RT}.
Activation energy calculation mixes joules and kilojoules without unit consistency. Ensure slope unit conversion explicitly yields EA in kJâmolâ»Â¹.
The concluding remark on exothermicity is incomplete and conceptually unclear; the student should discuss why a negative ÎH does not directly equate to measured EA.
Equipment uncertainty table is thorough, but there is no propagation of these uncertainties into the calculated activation energy, limiting insight into precision of EA.
The uncertainty analysis separates total, random, and systematic errors, demonstrating thoughtful consideration, but propagation through to EA is missing.
Comparison to a generic literature EA of 45 kJâmolâ»Â¹ lacks specificity to FeClâ catalysis, which may misrepresent systematic error magnitude.
The conclusion incorrectly states ârate of reaction decreasesâ with catalyst addition; it should note that reaction time decreases and rate increases with lower EA.
The evaluation table identifies strengths and weaknesses clearly, offering a balanced reflection on experimental design and data reliability.
The evaluation lists generic improvements but does not explain how delays in HâOâ handling quantitatively impact EA or rate constants.
